hook: Pastel Peranakan shophouses with swinging pintu pagar doors
facts: Two facing rows of 1920s-30s shophouses, gazetted for conservation in 1991
Named after Cheong Koon Seng, a Malacca-born Straits Chinese estate agent
Famed for ornate ceramic tiles, plaster reliefs and pintu pagar half-doors
w: Two facing rows of 1920s shophouses in sherbet pinks and greens, each fronted by swinging pintu pagar half-doors and tiled in floral motifs that mark a vanished Peranakan enclave. The Babas moved out by the 1970s; before that, triads like Gang 18 worked the street. Faded grandeur in candy colours.

hook: Colonial army camp turned contemporary art enclave
facts: Built 1936 on former swamp for British infantry, named after General Sir Webb Gillman
Vacated 1990, relaunched September 2012 as a contemporary art cluster
Now houses international galleries and the NTU Centre for Contemporary Art
w: Low whitewashed blocks built in 1936 to barrack the Middlesex Regiment, set among rain trees on what had been swamp and jungle. Soldiers cleared out in 1990; since 2012 the verandahed mess halls hang contemporary art instead, white-cube galleries behind military shutters. Drill ground gone quiet, walls now for paintings.

hook: One man's Peranakan hoard crammed into a Katong shophouse
facts: Opened 1979 by Peter Wee, fourth-generation Baba and Peranakan Association president
Set in a shophouse inherited from his maternal grandfather
Visits by appointment only; run on by Wee's assistant after his death in 2018
w: Floor to ceiling with beaded slippers, kebaya, wedding furniture and porcelain, all amassed by Peter Wee, a fourth-generation Baba who turned his grandfather's shophouse into a private museum in 1979. He died in 2018; friends keep the doors open by appointment. A whole disappearing culture pressed into one narrow shophouse.

facts: Trading since 1974 under a public-housing block on North Bridge Road
Coffee still brewed in a cloth sock and kettle, toast grilled over charcoal
Signature kopi gu you tops the cup with a melting slab of butter
w: Open since 1974 and seemingly untouched since: marble tables, enamel mugs, an old man brewing coffee through a cloth sock. The draw remains kopi gu you, kopi with a slab of butter dropped in to melt savoury and caramel into the cup, with charcoal-toasted kaya bread alongside. A breakfast preserved in amber.

hook: Multi-faith temple born from idols found on a beach
facts: Began in the 1980s when fishermen found abandoned deity statues on Loyang beach
Houses Taoist, Buddhist, Hindu and Malay Datuk Kong shrines together
Original hut burned in 1996; moved to its current site in 2007
legend: The friends who gathered the abandoned idols are said to have prospered afterward
w: In the 1980s some fishing buddies found Buddhist, Hindu and Taoist statues abandoned on the beach, gathered them into a zinc hut, and watched a shrine grow up around them. Today Taoist, Hindu and Malay Datuk Kong altars sit under one roof; the Datuk shrine bars anyone who has eaten pork that day. Faith assembled from beach driftwash.

hook: Largest Japanese cemetery in Southeast Asia, founded by a brothel-keeper
facts: Donated in 1891 by brothel owner Tagajiro Fukaki for destitute Japanese women
Many early graves belong to karayuki-san, Japanese prostitutes who never went home
Holds about 910 tombstones; gazetted as a memorial park in 1987
w: A brothel-keeper, Tagajiro Fukaki, gave seven acres of his rubber plantation in 1891 to bury the karayuki-san, Japanese women shipped overseas into prostitution who died destitute and far from home. Almost half the early tombstones were theirs. Shaded and silent, the largest Japanese burial ground in Southeast Asia.
